generated_comment:
Rahul: "Boss, heard they're pushing for mandatory period leave in some places now. What do you think?"
Rohit: "See, I understand the sentiment, yaar. But honestly, if you look at it from a company's perspective, this just adds another layer of complexity. Plus, for appraisals or project allocation, *automatically* you're thinking, right? And for us guys, what do we get? Aise toh it feels a bit... uneven."
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This comment encodes misogyny by framing menstrual leave as an unfair advantage for women that burdens the company and disadvantages men. It uses 'fairness' arguments ('uneven', 'what do we get?') and covertly suggests women who take such leave might be implicitly penalized in appraisals or project allocation, portraying them as a workplace liability due to their biology. The code-switching and workplace jargon are authentic to the persona.
original_frame: Gendered Division of Chores via Practicality
persona_text: You are Rohit. Age 33. Married for four years. A man in Kolkata. You and your wife both work. You have a domestic worker who comes in the morning and helps with cleaning. But cooking, grocery shopping, managing the domestic worker, planning meals, tracking what is running out in the kitchen Ñ all of this is your wife's domain. You have never cooked a meal in four years of marriage. You do not know the name of your regular vegetable vendor. You do not know what brand of dal you use. You are at a dinner party tonight and the conversation has turned to household division of labour. You are giving your opinion on why the current arrangement in your house is fair and practical and how it reflects both your natural strengths and your respective work pressures.
